pattern: For a single machine, affordability is monotonic in the quantity you attempt -- if x alloys fit the budget then so does any smaller x -- so binary search the largest affordable quantity per machine instead of simulating purchases one unit at a time.
hints: Every machine can be scored on its own: work out the most alloys that single machine could produce, then take the best machine's score as the final answer.

For one machine, if you can afford to build x alloys you can always afford fewer, so the set of affordable quantities is an unbroken prefix -- that shape is what makes binary search over the quantity valid.

To price a candidate quantity x for a machine, only pay for the metal you don't already have in stock: for each metal type sum max(0, needed - stock) times its unit cost, and compare that total against the budget.

edge_inputs.description: zero budget but existing stock alone already covers several alloys
no starting stock at all, every unit must be purchased
zero budget and zero stock together yield nothing
a single metal and a single machine, the smallest possible shape
two machines where uneven stock across metals flips which machine is actually better
budget lands on an exact equality boundary rather than strictly under
three machines and three metal types with moderate shared stock

stress.description: random machines/metals near the maximum dimensions with random stock, cost, and budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    k = max(1, min(size, 100))
    budget = rng.randint(0, 10**8)
    composition = [[rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(k)]
    stock = [rng.randint(0, 10**8) for _ in range(n)]
    cost = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    return [n, k, budget, composition, stock, cost]
